item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congrats on 400

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know its good when you question your morality….

+1

item.title: Re: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded: Can honestly say I’m honored! Thank you so much for the read!!! Well done!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
we find it hard to believe that any being under the age of ten has enough to power to, as some of your peers have put it, 'vaporize my head' despite the information you have provided.

I laughed so hard. Wait until you see the 3 years old that makes you go insane, the child that managed to destroy an entire building when it was a baby and that negotiating housefly.

A story about what happened within those two weeks would be a fun read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amazing, I love this article and I love this cannon.

+1

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
This was really fun to act out; I'm also adding this to the list of Tales that would be both simple to do yet fun and dramatic to do a live action short of. Just a thought for the future…

Audio Adaptation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah ethics, thou art a heartless hag.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some good lines in this, but it doesn't really go anywhere.

item.title: Re: Upvoted, but...
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the upvote and feedback! Actually, that's a really interesting idea, but not one that super fits my writing style. I tend to like bookends and nice, closed stories (or closed loops if I'm dealing with time) unless I plan on writing series. Open ends and loose threads bug me unless they're done really well, which I'm not certain I could always pull off. Thanks again!

item.title: Upvoted, but...
item.content:encoded: I did like this article, but I feel like it might have been better without the newspaper clipping at the end. I feel like it would be better with a more… open ended ending that doesn't exactly answer the question the title proposes- "Ethical?", leaving it up to the reader to decide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There we have it, the end answer that nobody wants to hear. Perhaps there should be a G.O.I group with this kind of ideology because we all know they're out there somewhere.
Yay! Thanks to everyone who has read, reviewed, critiqued and voted! (Even though technically it's still a little below 100 because of deleted accounts and such). Regardless, thank you everyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ouch.

Honestly, though, there are points at which I'm not sure the humane thing is to let humanity live …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Many thanks to Randomini for doing a reading of this here!! If you've not already heard it, go do so…it is very well done and you won't be sorry.

And now Kaktus did it as well as a Christmas present! Thank you! Link here.

Go. Give both of these a listen. You shall be much pleased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!! I appreciate it a lot!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like this. Really, really, really like this. It's a bit cliche, what with it being a review board and all, but to be honest, I don't care one bit; this was great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah that can be done. I feex…I think. If that isn't what you had in mind, feel free to correct me. Thanks for pointing that out Reject.

I hadn't realized it until looking at it later but it didn't look the best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
.

.

.

.

God damnit. Can these please be normal spaces? Use four @s together for a line break that doesn't look terrible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well thank you! I appreciate it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good work. I love ethical philosophy and this summarises pretty well the normative ethical arguments, introduced by the premise of the Foundation's methods and goals, and manages to give a human perspective on it from both the UN board and Dr. Feldon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. I hadn't really thought to explore there, at least here. Might be good fodder for a future tale. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm kind of neutral on it myself. There's a lot to like here,n but I feel as if it didn't go far enough. For instance, SCP-173. Why is it even kept around? Far as I know, no real data can or has ever been gained form it, and it's been killing people left and right who get near it. There are plenty or reasons to side with the GOC mindset and destroy items, or even if you do or do not have something that is destructive to human life.

The crux of the argument is that sometimes horrible things must be done for the greater good. Okay, go into more detail. Is SCP-173 worth destroying? How should D-Class be handled? Should Cain be allowed to speak about the religious implications is existence gives? There's a lot of good stuff this debate could talk about, but it felt like it fell short.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dissociative_drug

except Larry isn't a drug, it's a two-person llama costume wearing galoshes.

They were using Larry for recreational purposes, to achieve an effect similar to that caused by dissociative drugs. It was psychic-dissociative recreation: recreation that involved having their psyches dissociate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What exactly is psychic-dissociative recreation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Opinion: I think there's a difference between the study/discussion of ethics and moral philosophy in theory space, that is the space of all theoretical discussion on ethics by moral philosophers etc., and applied ethics, or the application of ethics in actual practice by organizations and other bodies composed of multiple individuals with stated goals.

In theory it's difficult or impossible to say what ethical paradigm is best, but ethics as applied within organizations is usually grounded in the practical goals of the body. One may subscribe to Kantian ethics and believe the Montauk Procedure is morally reprehensible regardless of consequence, and thus the protagonist of this tale is espousing nonsense about how his utilitarian philosophy is superior to whatever paradigms other bodies or individuals may believe to be correct. However, the stated goal of the Foundation is the protection of all humanity from the paranormal - implicitly this means that this goal is held to be the ultimate virtue by the Foundation. Within this framework, the ethics committee works to ensure that procedures do not overstep the bounds of what is necessary - it is not an organization for discussing the merits of moral philosophical paradigms in theory space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Predictable but still worth an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this canon, and I love this tale. It might not be the best one, but I do love the concept of people questioning the ethics of The Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with your assessment, but liked it enough nonetheless to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think virtue ehtics isn't that common now for some good reasons. Quickly summed up, because it is weird (that of course being only my perception). Also try applying it to Foundation moral: While this would sure be worth some laughs, I doubt the Foundation could justify much with it mainly because the Foundations virtues aren't the best to build moral up on them.

While the other problems you mention sure are present, one can dicuss them only when already decided between deontology and consequentialism.
Therefore I think as long Foundation ethcics on grand scale are the topic one can't avoid the consequentialism vs. deontology debate. When writing about some special topic inside Foundation ehtics it may be possible to apply some other ethical perspectives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just hope the New Ethics Committee discovered the limitations of their old ethical constraints before suspending Procedure 110-Montauk…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are plenty of other ethical problems. Deontology and utilitarianism aren't even necessarily in conflict. More strictly speaking it's usually deontology and consequentialism that are in conflict, and utilitarianism happens to be the most common form of consequentialism. But there's plenty of other ethical perspectives and axes; virtue ethics is relatively rare right now but has been historically quite important; there's absolutism vs. non-absolutism, objectivism vs. subjectivism, motive-agnostic vs. motive-conscious ethics…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree that this is basically about the debate between utilitarism and deontologogy but this can be said of nearly every problem concerning ethics. It's not that easy to derail from this axis as you call it, because there simply aren't any other systematic theories of ethic that would be applicable in any way to the Foundations doing. At least, I don't know of any.

On a sidenote I think it could be possible to justify Foundation ethics from a deontologists point of view, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. This story isn't bad. I like the dialogue. But the flow of it was very predictable.

I think there are more interesting ethical conflicts to explore. In this context, for example, the "inquisitors" could challenge Foundation ethics by attacking the underlying axioms. There are plenty of entities that the Foundation contains by very "cruel" means which are the only way to keep it contained - but that could be destroyed much more "humanely".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like Feldon's tonal shift from meek and helpless to rebellious and condemning is a bit rapid, but all the same I enjoyed this story. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't the UN already have the GOC?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I neutral-voted for largely the same reasons. The easy way out in terms of ethical debates regarding the Foundation is, basically, utilitarianism vs. deontology. And that's, like, boring. You can make it even easier and have the util be the "did what I had to do" guilty conscience hard men, and the deontology is the soft emotive/intuition-based ethical system. You don't really do the first but you definitely do the second.

I mean, I probably agree with the "message" of the story, which is that on large scales util is probably a better ethical model than deontology. But it doesn't really explore the deeper ethical implications of, like…everything that's going on. For once I want to see a story that doesn't operate on the deont/util axis. Go perpendicular to the axis. Fuck the axis, it's literally one-dimensional, and Voct's Ethics Committee Orientation and black white black white grey are okay but do we really have to keep exploring the line?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wasn't the intent.

The intent was: the Foundation makes choices largely deemed unethical, but not because their view of ethics is different, but because their view of scope is different. At most, the average person makes a choice that affects the handful of people nearest them, with leaders making choices for countries and so on. The Foundation has to look, literally, for what good benefits all people, or most people, of every country, and therefore the definition of ethics is difficult to ascertain. Essentially, until you walk a mile in another's shoes…

But to each their own. Thanks for the feedback at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, as near as I can tell, this boils down to "Your simplistic non-Foundation ethics are naive and harmful". This whole thing reads as terribly smug straw-manning. Downvoted accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for this canon, and probably that's why I consider this a solid brick of gold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sent a PM to Mann and Silber, a couple of the founders, asking if they minded if I linked there. I will when/if I get permission.

E: Mann PM'd me and said that he thought it fits, so, I'll go ahead and link it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Link in the canon's hub with who wrote it (yourself, in this case). I don't think there is a Broken masquerade tag.

Liked it in the forum. Love what you've done with it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very, very, well written. Great job! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to all the folks who looked this over in the forums and in chat! Too many of y'all to list, so feel free to list yourselves below, assuming this stays.

Also, since this is meant to fit into the Broken Masquerade canon, do I need to do anything to it/link it from there as well/special tags?
